file_name: Sirico et al. - 2024 - Implementing vision transformers in dermatological practice a web application for melanoma screenin.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a Vision Transformer (ViT) Large model, a deep learning architecture, which was fine-tuned on ISIC 2019 and 2020 dermatoscopic image datasets to classify images as 'Melanoma' or 'Non-Melanoma'. The results, including precision, recall, and F1-scores, were obtained using this AI model for melanoma screening.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development of a web application for melanoma screening using a fine-tuned Vision Transformer model. It details specific methods including dataset preparation from ISIC 2019/2020 and MEDNODE, model fine-tuning, and validation results with specific metrics (precision, recall, F1-score) in Tables 1 and 2. Although published in conference proceedings (Ital-IA 2024), it reports new technical results and validation rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Implementing Vision Transformers in Dermatological Practice: A Web Application for Melanoma Screening
journal: Ital-IA 2024: 4th National Conference on Artificial Intelligence
brief_summary: This study developed a web-based application for melanoma screening using a fine-tuned Vision Transformer (ViT) Large model to classify dermatoscopic images as melanoma or non-melanoma. The model was trained on datasets from the ISIC Challenges of 2019 and 2020 and validated on the distinct MEDNODE dataset to assess generalization. Evaluation on the ISIC test set yielded an F1-score of 0.87 for melanoma and 0.85 for non-melanoma cases. On the MEDNODE dataset, the model achieved an F1-score of 0.82 for melanoma and 0.90 for non-melanoma cases. The resulting Streamlit-based application provides an intuitive interface for users to upload images and receive real-time diagnostic predictions, aiming to democratize access to early melanoma detection tools.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the two binary classification targets used in the ViT model experiments on ISIC 2019/2020 and MEDNODE datasets.
The comparator class in the binary classification task, encompassing all non-melanoma skin lesions present in the ISIC and MEDNODE datasets.

all_task_types.details: The model performs binary classification of dermatoscopic images into two discrete classes: Melanoma and Non-Melanoma. The study reports classification metrics including precision, recall, and F1-score for both classes on the ISIC 2019/2020 test set and the MEDNODE dataset. While the web application visualizes the probability of melanoma presence, the primary task is discrete class prediction.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2019 and ISIC 2020 Challenge Datasets
datasets_used.description: A synthesized dataset constructed from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) Challenge datasets of 2019 and 2020. The 'Melanoma' class images were sourced from both ISIC 2019 and ISIC 2020, while the 'No-Melanoma' class images were sourced exclusively from ISIC 2019 to ensure balanced classes.

use.training: Used to fine-tune the pre-trained Vision Transformer (ViT) Large model.
use.testing: Used to evaluate the model's performance (ISIC Test Set).

datasets_used.name: MEDNODE Dataset
datasets_used.description: An entirely different, distinct dataset used to assess the model's generalization capability and robustness in real-world scenarios. It is distinct in composition and image characteristics from the ISIC datasets.

use.testing: Used for external validation to assess generalization and robustness.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To visualize and quantify the model's accuracy in classifying cases, detailing true positives, true negatives, false positives, and false negatives for both Melanoma and Non-Melanoma classes on the ISIC and MEDNODE datasets.
To elucidate the model's diagnostic ability across different thresholds, showcasing the trade-off between sensitivity (true positive rate) and specificity (true negative rate).
To evaluate the performance of the classification model for each class (Melanoma and Non-Melanoma) on the ISIC test set and the MEDNODE dataset.
To indicate the number of instances (samples) used to calculate the precision, recall, and F1-score for each class.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using classification metrics (precision, recall, F1-score) and visualizations (confusion matrices, ROC curves) without statistical significance testing.

ISIC Test Set.notes: The model demonstrated substantial precision, recall, and F1-score metrics across both categories. The consistency in precision across both classes, paired with balanced F1-scores, attests to the model's robustness. The elevated recall for the Melanoma class indicates a particular strength in reducing false negatives.

MEDNODE Dataset.notes: The model demonstrated high capability in correctly identifying non-melanoma cases with a high recall rate (0.98), minimizing the risk of false negatives. For the Melanoma category, the precision was 0.96, but the recall was 0.71, highlighting a potential area for improvement in capturing all true melanoma cases. The model's performance on this distinct dataset enhances its credibility as a robust diagnostic tool.

architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained on general vision tasks by Google

training_details.hardware: AWS p3.2xlarge, Intel Xeon E5-2686 v4, 64GB RAM, Tesla V100-SXM2 16GB VRAM

preprocessing_techniques: Dataset synthesis from ISIC 2019 and ISIC 2020 challenges to create balanced classes (Melanoma and No-Melanoma)
Class balancing by sourcing 'No-Melanoma' images exclusively from ISIC 2019 and 'Melanoma' images from both ISIC 2019 and ISIC 2020
conclusions: The fine-tuned Vision Transformer (ViT) Large model demonstrates robust performance in classifying melanoma versus non-melanoma lesions, achieving an F1-score of 0.87 for melanoma and 0.85 for non-melanoma on the ISIC test set.

The model exhibits strong generalization capabilities on the distinct MEDNODE dataset, achieving an F1-score of 0.82 for melanoma and 0.90 for non-melanoma, confirming its reliability across different imaging sources.

The application highlights a trade-off in melanoma detection on the MEDNODE dataset, where high precision (0.96) was balanced by lower recall (0.71), indicating a need for further refinement to improve sensitivity and reduce false negatives.

The developed Streamlit-based web application provides an accessible, user-friendly interface for preliminary melanoma screening, potentially democratizing access to early detection tools for both medical professionals and the general public.

The inference phase of the model requires no dedicated high-performance GPU hardware, allowing for deployment in a wide range of environments and facilitating broader clinical adoption without high computational costs.
The study underscores the viability of Vision Transformer models in medical imaging and their potential to enhance diagnostic accuracy and patient outcomes through timely intervention.
important_remarks: The study is limited by a small external validation dataset (MEDNODE, n=170), which may not fully represent the diversity of real-world clinical populations.

The model exhibits a significant trade-off in melanoma detection on the MEDNODE dataset, with high precision (0.96) but notably lower recall (0.71), indicating a risk of false negatives in this specific validation set.

The training dataset was synthetically constructed by balancing classes (sourcing 'No-Melanoma' exclusively from ISIC 2019 and 'Melanoma' from both ISIC 2019 and 2020), which may introduce selection bias compared to natural prevalence.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was reported; performance was evaluated solely using classification metrics (precision, recall, F1-score).
The paper does not report data leakage risks or detailed preprocessing/augmentation techniques beyond class balancing, limiting reproducibility of the training pipeline.
The application is designed for preliminary screening and democratizing access, but the paper does not provide evidence of clinical utility or impact on patient outcomes in a real-world setting.
The inference phase requires no dedicated high-performance GPU, suggesting potential for broad deployment, but this claim is not supported by latency or throughput benchmarks.
